questions.question: How many private hole cards is each player dealt in Texas Hold'em?

explanations.A: Correct. Each player is dealt exactly two private hole cards.
explanations.B: One hole card appears in some exotic games, not standard Hold'em.
explanations.C: Three hole cards is not a Hold'em deal.
explanations.D: Four hole cards is the game of Omaha, not Hold'em.

questions.question: How many community cards are shown in total across the flop, turn, and river?

explanations.A: Three is only the flop, before the turn and river.
explanations.B: Correct. Flop (3) + turn (1) + river (1) = 5 community cards.
explanations.C: Four counts the flop and turn but omits the river.
explanations.D: Six exceeds the five shared cards Hold'em deals.

questions.question: A standard deck used for Texas Hold'em contains how many cards?

explanations.A: 48 would be missing four cards from a standard deck.
explanations.B: 50 is not a standard deck size.
explanations.C: Correct. A standard deck has 52 cards and no jokers in Hold'em.
explanations.D: 54 includes two jokers, which Hold'em does not use.

questions.question: How many suits are in a standard deck?

explanations.A: Two would ignore half of the four suits.
explanations.B: Three misses one suit.
explanations.C: Five is one more suit than a deck contains.
explanations.D: Correct. The four suits are spades, hearts, diamonds, and clubs.

questions.question: How many cards of each rank (for example, kings) are in the deck?

explanations.A: Correct. Each rank has four cards, one per suit.
explanations.B: One would mean a single king total, but each rank repeats per suit.
explanations.C: Two undercounts the number of suits.
explanations.D: 13 is the number of ranks, not the cards per rank.

questions.question: Before showdown, who is allowed to see your hole cards?

explanations.A: Opponents only see your cards if you reach showdown and table them.
explanations.B: Correct. Hole cards are private to you until showdown.
explanations.C: The dealer does not look at your hole cards.
explanations.D: No opponent sees your hole cards based on seat order.

questions.question: Which is the highest-ranking hand in poker?

explanations.A: Four of a kind is strong but loses to any straight flush.
explanations.B: A full house ranks below four of a kind and below a flush only in the other direction; it cannot beat a royal flush.
explanations.C: Correct. A royal flush (A-K-Q-J-T of one suit) is the best possible hand.
explanations.D: A straight flush is second best; the royal is the highest straight flush.

questions.question: Order these from strongest to weakest: flush, straight, full house.
options: Straight, then flush, then full house
Flush, then full house, then straight
Full house, then straight, then flush
Full house, then flush, then straight

explanations.A: A straight is the weakest of the three, not the strongest.
explanations.B: A flush does not beat a full house.
explanations.C: A straight does not beat a flush.
explanations.D: Correct. A full house beats a flush, and a flush beats a straight.

questions.question: Does a flush beat a straight?
options: Yes, a flush beats a straight
No, a straight beats a flush
Only if the flush is ace-high

explanations.A: Correct. A flush is harder to make and outranks a straight.
explanations.B: This reverses the ranking; a flush is higher than a straight.
explanations.C: Different hand classes do not tie by rank.
explanations.D: Any flush beats any straight, regardless of its high card.

questions.question: Which ranks higher: three of a kind or two pair?

explanations.A: Two pair ranks below three of a kind.
explanations.B: Correct. Three of a kind beats two pair.
explanations.C: They are distinct ranks, not equal.
explanations.D: Suits never break the ranking between these hands.

questions.question: The phrase 'aces full of kings' describes which hand?

explanations.A: That would be called 'kings full of aces.'
explanations.B: A flush has no trips or pairs described this way.
explanations.C: Correct. A full house with three aces and two kings is 'aces full of kings.'
explanations.D: Four aces is quads, not a full house.

questions.question: The lowest possible straight, A-2-3-4-5, is nicknamed the:

explanations.A: Broadway is the highest straight, T-J-Q-K-A.
explanations.B: A royal flush is the top straight flush, not the lowest straight.
explanations.C: A gutshot is a draw, not a completed straight.
explanations.D: Correct. A-2-3-4-5 with the ace playing low is the 'wheel' or 'bicycle.'

questions.question: Which five cards form 'Broadway,' the highest straight?

explanations.A: Correct. T-J-Q-K-A is the ace-high straight known as Broadway.
explanations.B: This king-high straight is high but not the highest.
explanations.C: A-2-3-4-5 is the lowest straight, the wheel.
explanations.D: Straights cannot wrap around the ace, so K-A-2 is not connected.

questions.question: How many cards make up a final poker hand at showdown?

explanations.A: Four cards is not a complete poker hand.
explanations.B: Correct. The best five-card combination determines the hand.
explanations.C: Six is more than a poker hand uses.
explanations.D: You choose the best five of the seven available, not all seven.

questions.question: From your 2 hole cards and 5 board cards, you build your hand using:
options: Any five of the seven cards

explanations.A: You are not required to use both hole cards in Hold'em.
explanations.B: You may use the board, but you may also use hole cards.
explanations.C: Correct. You make the best five-card hand from any of the seven cards.
explanations.D: There is no rule fixing exactly three board cards.

questions.question: 'Playing the board' means your best five-card hand:
options: Uses both of your hole cards
Is made entirely of the community cards

explanations.A: Using both hole cards is not playing the board.
explanations.B: Using a hole card that helps is not playing the board.
explanations.C: A draw is not a made hand.
explanations.D: Correct. The five community cards alone form your best hand.

questions.question: You hold A-K. The board runs out Q-J-T-2-7 with no flush possible. Your best five-card hand is:

explanations.A: Correct. A-K combine with Q-J-T to make Broadway, the ace-high straight.
explanations.B: You actually have a straight, which is far stronger than one pair.
explanations.C: A king-high straight needs a 9; your ace extends it to a higher straight.
explanations.D: No five cards share a suit here, and your two cards cannot make a flush.

questions.question: A 'set' specifically means three of a kind made with:
options: One hole card plus a pair on the board
A pocket pair plus one matching board card

explanations.A: That describes 'trips,' not a set.
explanations.B: Correct. A set is a pocket pair that pairs a board card.
explanations.C: Three matching board cards are shared by everyone, not a set.
explanations.D: You only ever hold two hole cards in Hold'em.

questions.question: Two players each hold a pair of aces. The winner is decided by:

explanations.A: Suits do not rank hands in standard Hold'em.
explanations.B: It only splits if the full five-card hands are identical.
explanations.C: Correct. The highest unmatched side card, the kicker, breaks the tie.
explanations.D: Position has no bearing on hand strength.

questions.question: What is the correct order of the betting rounds?

explanations.A: Preflop always comes before the flop.
explanations.B: The turn comes after the flop, not before it.
explanations.C: The river is always the final street, after the turn.
explanations.D: Correct. Action goes preflop, then flop, turn, and river.

questions.question: How many community cards are revealed on the flop?

explanations.A: Correct. The flop reveals three community cards at once.
explanations.B: One card is the turn or river, not the flop.
explanations.C: Two cards are never dealt as a single street.
explanations.D: Four is the board total only after the turn.

questions.question: The single community card dealt after the flop is the:

explanations.A: The river is the last community card, dealt after the turn.
explanations.B: Correct. The turn is the fourth community card, dealt after the flop.
explanations.C: A burn card is discarded face-down, not part of the board.
explanations.D: Showdown is when hands are revealed, not a card.

questions.question: The fifth and final community card is called the:

explanations.A: The turn is the fourth card, dealt before the river.
explanations.B: The flop is the first three community cards.
explanations.C: Correct. The river is the fifth and final community card.
explanations.D: 'Door card' is a stud term, not used in Hold'em.

questions.question: How many betting rounds occur in a typical Hold'em hand?

explanations.A: Two would omit half of the streets.
explanations.B: Three misses one street.
explanations.C: Five is one more round than Hold'em has.
explanations.D: Correct. Preflop, flop, turn, and river make four betting rounds.

questions.question: 'Preflop' refers to the betting that happens:
options: Before any community cards are dealt

explanations.A: Correct. Preflop betting happens before any community cards appear.
explanations.B: Betting after the flop is the flop round, not preflop.
explanations.C: Betting after the river is the final round.
explanations.D: Showdown has no betting.

questions.question: Just before dealing the flop, the dealer typically:

explanations.A: No additional hole cards are dealt after preflop.
explanations.B: Correct. One card is burned before the flop to guard against marked cards.
explanations.C: The deck is not reshuffled in the middle of a hand.
explanations.D: The river comes much later in the hand.

questions.question: Across a full hand (flop, turn, river), how many cards are burned?

explanations.A: One burn covers only the flop.
explanations.B: Two burns would omit one street.
explanations.C: Correct. One card is burned before each of the flop, turn, and river.
explanations.D: Five matches the board cards, not the burns.

questions.question: Hole cards are dealt starting with which player?
options: The small blind (first seat left of the button)

explanations.A: The button receives the last card on each pass, not the first.
explanations.B: The big blind is dealt after the small blind.
explanations.C: UTG is dealt later in the rotation.
explanations.D: Correct. Dealing begins with the small blind and moves clockwise.

questions.question: In a $1/$2 cash game, the small blind is:

explanations.A: Correct. The first number, $1, is the small blind.
explanations.B: $2 is the big blind, the second number.
explanations.C: $0.50 is not posted in this structure.
explanations.D: $3 is not a blind in a $1/$2 game.

questions.question: In a $2/$5 cash game, the big blind is:

explanations.A: $2 is the small blind here.
explanations.B: Correct. The second number, $5, is the big blind.
explanations.C: $7 is the sum of both blinds, not the big blind.
explanations.D: $1 is not posted in this structure.

questions.question: Who normally posts the small blind?
options: The player two seats right of the button
The player immediately to the left of the button

explanations.A: The button is to the right of the small blind.
explanations.B: That seat is not the small blind in standard order.
explanations.C: Correct. The small blind sits directly left of the button.
explanations.D: UTG sits to the left of the big blind, not in the blinds.

questions.question: Blinds are best described as:
options: Optional bets a player may skip
Bets made only on the river
Forced bets posted before cards are dealt

explanations.A: Blinds are mandatory, not optional.
explanations.B: Blinds are posted preflop, not on the river.
explanations.C: A side pot comes from unequal all-ins, unrelated to blinds.
explanations.D: Correct. Blinds are mandatory bets that seed the pot before the deal.

questions.question: If everyone just calls the big blind preflop, the big blind may:

explanations.A: Correct. With no raise in front, the big blind can check or raise their option.
explanations.B: The big blind is not forced to fold a free option.
explanations.C: Raising is allowed but not required.
explanations.D: The big blind acts last preflop in an unraised pot.

questions.question: A 'straddle' is:
options: A mandatory ante for all players
A voluntary blind, usually twice the big blind, posted before the deal
A bet made only on the river

explanations.A: An ante is a separate forced bet, usually for everyone.
explanations.B: Correct. A straddle is an optional blind, often double the big blind, that buys last preflop action.
explanations.C: A straddle is posted preflop, not on the river.
explanations.D: A straddle is a bet, not a fold.

questions.question: In tournaments, the blinds typically:
options: Stay the same for the entire event
Decrease as the event goes on

explanations.A: Fixed blinds describe cash games, not tournaments.
explanations.B: Blinds rise, not fall, in tournaments.
explanations.C: Correct. Tournament blinds rise on a schedule to force action over time.
explanations.D: Blinds are still posted each hand in tournaments.

questions.question: An ante is:
options: A blind posted only by the button
A small forced bet contributed toward the pot

explanations.A: Antes are not limited to the button in standard formats.
explanations.B: Antes are forced, not voluntary.
explanations.C: Antes are posted before the hand, not at showdown.
explanations.D: Correct. An ante is a forced contribution that builds the pot, common in later tournament levels.

questions.question: Why is the button the most powerful position?
options: It acts last on every postflop street
It never has to post blinds
It is dealt its cards first

explanations.A: Correct. Acting last postflop gives you maximum information before deciding.
explanations.B: The button avoids blinds on its own hand but still pays them as it rotates.
explanations.C: Deal order is not a strategic advantage.
explanations.D: Ties are split by hand strength, not by position.

questions.question: Being 'in position' on an opponent means you:
options: Act after them on postflop streets

explanations.A: Acting before an opponent is being out of position.
explanations.B: Correct. In position means you act after your opponent postflop.
explanations.C: Position does not force any specific action.
explanations.D: Position is about action order, not the blinds.

questions.question: The 'cutoff' is the seat:
options: Immediately to the left of the button
Two seats to the left of the button
Immediately to the right of the button
The same as the big blind

explanations.A: The seat left of the button is the small blind.
explanations.B: Two seats left of the button is the big blind.
explanations.C: Correct. The cutoff sits directly to the right of the button.
explanations.D: The big blind is in the blinds, not the cutoff.

questions.question: 'Under the gun' (UTG) is the player who:
options: Acts first preflop in a full-ring or 6-max game

explanations.A: The button or big blind option acts later preflop.
explanations.B: UTG is left of the big blind, not the blind itself.
explanations.C: The button is a late position, not UTG.
explanations.D: Correct. UTG, seated left of the big blind, is first to act preflop.

questions.question: Postflop, the betting action begins with:
options: The first active player to the left of the button

explanations.A: Correct. Postflop action starts with the first remaining player left of the button.
explanations.B: The button acts last postflop.
explanations.C: UTG ordering applies preflop, not postflop.
explanations.D: Order follows seating, not who bet last.

questions.question: Preflop, with blinds posted, the first player to act is:
options: The player to the left of the big blind (UTG)

explanations.A: The small blind already has a forced bet and acts later preflop.
explanations.B: Correct. Preflop action starts to the left of the big blind, with UTG.
explanations.C: The big blind acts last preflop in an unraised pot.
explanations.D: The button acts late preflop, not first.

questions.question: In a heads-up (two-player) hand, preflop the button:
options: Acts first, because the button is the small blind

explanations.A: Heads-up, the button acts last only postflop, not preflop.
explanations.B: Heads-up, the button posts the small blind, not the big blind.
explanations.C: Correct. Heads-up, the button posts the small blind and acts first preflop.
explanations.D: The button is never forced to fold.

questions.question: In heads-up play, postflop the button acts:

explanations.A: Heads-up, the big blind acts first postflop.
explanations.B: The button acts on every postflop street it reaches.
explanations.C: The button acts on all postflop streets, not just the river.
explanations.D: Correct. Heads-up, the button acts last on each postflop street.

questions.question: 6-max: UTG opens, CO calls, BTN calls, SB folds, BB calls. Who acts first on the flop?

explanations.A: Correct. The big blind is the first active seat to the left of the button postflop.
explanations.B: The button acts last among the remaining players.
explanations.C: UTG acts after the big blind postflop.
explanations.D: The cutoff acts late postflop, not first.

questions.question: Facing no bet, which action keeps you in the hand for free?

explanations.A: Folding gives up the hand entirely.
explanations.B: Correct. Checking passes the action without adding chips.
explanations.C: There is nothing to call when no bet has been made.
explanations.D: Raising would put chips in voluntarily.

questions.question: To 'call' means to:
options: Increase the size of the bet
Match the amount of the current bet
Pass when there is no bet

explanations.A: Increasing the bet is a raise.
explanations.B: Giving up your hand is a fold.
explanations.C: Correct. Calling matches the current bet to stay in the hand.
explanations.D: Passing with no bet is a check.

questions.question: A 'raise' is an action that:
options: Can only be done on the river
Increases the amount opponents must call

explanations.A: Matching exactly is a call.
explanations.B: Surrendering the hand is a fold.
explanations.C: Raises can occur on any betting round.
explanations.D: Correct. A raise increases the bet, forcing others to call more or fold.

questions.question: Folding means you:
options: Forfeit your hand and any claim to the pot
Stay in the hand for free

explanations.A: Correct. Folding surrenders your cards and your claim to the pot.
explanations.B: Staying in for free is checking.
explanations.C: Folding lets you avoid matching the bet.
explanations.D: Folded players cannot win any pot.

questions.question: A 'check-raise' occurs when a player:
options: Folds and then re-enters the hand
Checks, then raises after an opponent bets
Bets and then folds to a raise
Calls two streets in a row

explanations.A: You cannot re-enter a hand after folding.
explanations.B: Correct. You check first, then raise once an opponent bets behind you.
explanations.C: Betting then folding is not a check-raise.
explanations.D: Calling is passive and is not a raise.

questions.question: Facing a bet, your three options are to:

explanations.A: You cannot check when you are facing a bet.
explanations.B: Those are not in-turn options when facing a bet.
explanations.C: Correct. Against a bet you may fold, call, or raise.
explanations.D: Those are dealer mechanics, not player actions.

questions.question: A 'string bet' (reaching back for more chips after your first motion) is:
options: Not allowed; you must declare or move chips in one motion

explanations.A: String bets are disallowed to prevent angle-shooting.
explanations.B: A single clean motion is required, not a string of motions.
explanations.C: The no-string-bet rule applies on every street.
explanations.D: Correct. You must state your raise or place the chips in a single motion.

questions.question: At a casino, a clear verbal declaration of 'raise' made in turn is:
options: Binding; you must complete the raise

explanations.A: Correct. A clear in-turn verbal declaration commits you to that action.
explanations.B: Verbal action in turn is binding, not optional.
explanations.C: It is a commitment, not a suggestion.
explanations.D: Dealers enforce binding verbal declarations.

questions.question: In no-limit Hold'em, the minimum raise must be at least:
options: Double the big blind in all cases
The size of the previous bet or raise
One chip more than the call
Half the size of the pot

explanations.A: It is not fixed at double the big blind on later streets.
explanations.B: Correct. A legal raise must be at least as large as the previous bet or raise increment.
explanations.C: A one-chip bump is not a legal full raise.
explanations.D: Half-pot is a sizing choice, not the minimum-raise rule.

questions.question: In no-limit Hold'em, the most you can bet at once is:
options: Only the size of the pot

explanations.A: A pot-sized cap describes pot-limit, not no-limit.
explanations.B: There is no fixed cap at double the big blind.
explanations.C: Correct. In no-limit you may bet up to your whole stack.
explanations.D: You wager the chips in front of you, not 'buy-ins.'

questions.question: In pot-limit Hold'em, the largest legal bet equals:
options: Your entire stack at any time
The current size of the pot

explanations.A: Betting your whole stack any time is no-limit.
explanations.B: The big blind is a minimum reference, not the maximum.
explanations.C: The cap is the pot size, not strictly twice the last bet.
explanations.D: Correct. In pot-limit, the maximum bet is the size of the pot.

questions.question: In fixed-limit Hold'em, bets and raises are made in:
options: Any amount up to your stack

explanations.A: Correct. Fixed-limit uses set bet sizes for each street.
explanations.B: Any amount up to your stack is no-limit.
explanations.C: Pot multiples describe pot-limit.
explanations.D: Sizes are fixed, not random.

questions.question: At showdown, the pot is awarded to:
options: The last player to make a bet
The best five-card hand among players still in

explanations.A: Betting last does not by itself win a contested showdown.
explanations.B: Correct. The best five-card hand wins the pot at showdown.
explanations.C: The button has no claim by position.
explanations.D: Show order does not decide the winner.

questions.question: If you bet the river and get called, to win you must:

explanations.A: You cannot claim the pot by mucking when called.
explanations.B: You did not fold; you were called.
explanations.C: Correct. When called, you must show your hand to win.
explanations.D: Blinds are posted before the hand, not at showdown.

questions.question: A player who folded on the turn:
options: Can still win with the best hand
Is out of the hand and cannot win the pot

explanations.A: Folding forfeits any claim, regardless of hand strength.
explanations.B: Folded players do not show at showdown.
explanations.C: Chips already bet stay in the pot.
explanations.D: Correct. Once you fold, you cannot win the pot.

questions.question: When two players have identical best five-card hands, the pot is:
options: Carried over to the next hand

explanations.A: Correct. Identical best hands split the pot evenly.
explanations.B: The button has no special claim to a tied pot.
explanations.C: Aggression does not break a true tie.
explanations.D: Pots are awarded that hand, not carried over.

questions.question: When there is no betting on the river, showdown order generally starts with:
options: The first active player left of the button, clockwise
Whoever asks to see a hand first

explanations.A: With no river bet, there is no last aggressor to lead.
explanations.B: Correct. With no river bet, cards are shown starting left of the button.
explanations.C: The button shows in turn, not necessarily first.
explanations.D: Show order follows seating rules, not requests.

questions.question: Side pots are created when:
options: All stacks at the table are equal
The board pairs on the turn
A player is all-in for less while others keep betting

explanations.A: Equal stacks need no side pot.
explanations.B: A paired board does not create side pots.
explanations.C: Correct. An all-in for less than others' bets splits off a side pot.
explanations.D: Blinds simply start the action; they do not create side pots.

questions.question: An all-in player who could not cover the later betting can win:
options: Both the main and the side pot
Only the main pot they contributed to

explanations.A: They are not eligible for chips beyond what they covered.
explanations.B: They did not contribute to the side pot, so they cannot win it.
explanations.C: They can still win the main pot with the best hand.
explanations.D: Correct. An all-in player can win only the main pot they matched.

questions.question: A is all-in for $40. B and C each match $40 and bet an extra $60 between them. The $60 forms:
options: A side pot only B and C can win
Part of the main pot A can win
A pot that A wins automatically

explanations.A: Correct. The extra $60 is a side pot contested only by B and C.
explanations.B: The first $40 from each forms the main pot; the extra is separate.
explanations.C: A cannot win chips beyond the amount they covered.
explanations.D: Live bets are not refunded mid-hand.

questions.question: An all-in raise that is smaller than a full legal raise:
options: May not reopen the betting for players who already acted

explanations.A: A sub-minimum all-in does not always reopen action.
explanations.B: Correct. An all-in short of a full raise often does not reopen action to those who already acted.
explanations.C: The hand continues; it does not end.
explanations.D: An all-in for one's whole stack is legal even if under a full raise.

questions.question: Under table-stakes rules, a player may wager:
options: As much extra cash as they want mid-hand
Money pulled from their pocket anytime
Only the chips they had on the table at the start of the hand

explanations.A: You cannot add cash to your wager mid-hand.
explanations.B: Reaching into your pocket mid-hand is not allowed.
explanations.C: Correct. Under table stakes, only chips in play at the start of the hand can be wagered.
explanations.D: You only wager your own chips.

questions.question: A player who is all-in:
options: Must fold to any later bet
Cannot be forced out and stays eligible for the pot they are in

explanations.A: An all-in player has no chips left to fold with.
explanations.B: They can still win at showdown.
explanations.C: They remain eligible for the main pot.
explanations.D: Correct. An all-in player cannot be bet out and contests the pot(s) they are in at showdown.

questions.question: Stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) is calculated as:
options: Effective stack divided by the pot
Pot divided by the effective stack
Big blind divided by the pot

explanations.A: Correct. SPR equals the effective stack divided by the size of the pot.
explanations.B: That inverts the ratio.
explanations.C: The big blind is not part of the SPR formula.
explanations.D: SPR divides; it does not multiply.

questions.question: Effective stacks are $300 and the flop pot is $30. The SPR is:

explanations.A: An SPR of 3 would require about a $100 pot.
explanations.B: Correct. $300 divided by $30 equals 10.
explanations.C: 30 confuses the pot size with the ratio.
explanations.D: 0.1 inverts the calculation.

questions.question: A low SPR (around 1 to 3) usually means:
options: There is lots of room to maneuver postflop
Players are close to committed and stacks go in quickly

explanations.A: Low SPR means little room, not a lot.
explanations.B: Blinds still set the structure of the hand.
explanations.C: Correct. With a low SPR, strong top pairs and overpairs are often committing.
explanations.D: Implied odds shrink as the SPR falls.

questions.question: A high SPR (say 10 or more) tends to:
options: Favor more maneuvering and stronger hands to stack off

explanations.A: High SPR allows multiple streets, not instant all-ins.
explanations.B: Position matters more when stacks are deep.
explanations.C: Draws gain value with deeper implied odds.
explanations.D: Correct. A deep SPR rewards patience and requires stronger hands to commit.

questions.question: Pot odds compare:
options: The cost to call versus the size of the pot you could win
Your stack to the size of the blinds
Your hand strength to the board texture
The rake to the total pot

explanations.A: Correct. Pot odds weigh the price of a call against the reward in the pot.
explanations.B: Stack-to-blinds is a different measure.
explanations.C: Comparing hand to board is hand reading, not pot odds.
explanations.D: The rake is a fee, not the pot-odds comparison.

questions.question: The pot is $100 and your opponent bets $50. You call $50 to win $150. The equity you need to break even is about:

explanations.A: 50% would apply only if you risked as much as the total reward.
explanations.B: Correct. $50 divided by ($150 + $50) equals 25%.
explanations.C: 33% corresponds to facing a pot-sized bet, not a half-pot bet.
explanations.D: 10% is far too low for this price.

questions.question: Calling $50 to win a $150 pot offers odds of:

explanations.A: 1-to-1 would be calling $50 to win $50.
explanations.B: 2-to-1 would be calling $50 to win $100.
explanations.C: Correct. $150 to $50 reduces to 3-to-1 odds.
explanations.D: 5-to-1 would be calling $50 to win $250.

questions.question: An opponent makes a pot-sized bet. The equity you need to call is about:

explanations.A: 20% is too generous a requirement for a pot-sized bet.
explanations.B: 25% corresponds to a half-pot bet, not a pot-sized one.
explanations.C: 50% applies to an over-bet much larger than the pot.
explanations.D: Correct. A pot-sized bet lays you 2-to-1, so you need roughly 33% equity.

questions.question: In poker, 'outs' are:
options: Unseen cards that improve your hand to likely the best
The chips sitting in the side pot

explanations.A: Correct. Outs are the remaining cards that complete or improve your hand.
explanations.B: Folded players are not 'outs.'
explanations.C: Side-pot chips are unrelated to outs.
explanations.D: Burn cards are discards, not outs.

questions.question: You have four cards to a flush after the flop. How many outs complete it?

explanations.A: Four would be the count for a gutshot, not a flush draw.
explanations.B: Correct. With 13 cards in a suit and 4 already seen, 9 outs remain.
explanations.C: Seven undercounts the cards left in the suit.
explanations.D: 13 is the full suit before removing the four you can see.

questions.question: An open-ended straight draw has how many outs?

explanations.A: Four outs is a gutshot, completing only one end.
explanations.B: Six is not the standard open-ended count.
explanations.C: Correct. Two ranks complete the straight, with 4 cards each, for 8 outs.
explanations.D: 15 outs is a combined flush-plus-straight draw.

questions.question: A gutshot (inside) straight draw has how many outs?

explanations.A: Two outs is closer to a set-to-quads draw.
explanations.B: Eight outs is an open-ended straight draw.
explanations.C: Nine outs is a flush draw.
explanations.D: Correct. Only one rank fills the inside gap, giving 4 outs.

questions.question: The 'rule of 4' estimates your chance to hit by the river (from the flop) as:

explanations.A: Correct. With two cards to come, multiply your outs by about 4 for a quick percentage.
explanations.B: Dividing badly understates your equity.
explanations.C: Outs times 2 estimates only one card to come.
explanations.D: Adding 4 is not the rule of 4.

questions.question: With one card to come (turn to river), a quick equity estimate is:

explanations.A: Times 4 is for two cards to come, not one.
explanations.B: Correct. Multiply your outs by about 2 for a single remaining card.
explanations.C: Dividing underestimates the draw.
explanations.D: Times 10 wildly overstates the draw.

questions.question: A flush draw (9 outs) on the flop has roughly what chance to complete by the river?

explanations.A: 10% badly understates a 9-out draw.
explanations.B: 20% is closer to a single street, not two cards.
explanations.C: Correct. About 35% (rule of 4: 9 times 4 is roughly 36%).
explanations.D: 70% is far too high for a flush draw.

questions.question: The chance of being dealt a specific pocket pair (such as pocket aces) is about:

explanations.A: 1 in 17 is the chance of any pocket pair.
explanations.B: 1 in 1326 is a single exact two-card combo, not a pair.
explanations.C: 1 in 52 is far too frequent.
explanations.D: Correct. A specific pair like aces is 6 of 1326 combos, about 1 in 221.

questions.question: The chance of being dealt any pocket pair is about:

explanations.A: Correct. There are 13 pairs times 6 combos, 78 of 1326, about 1 in 17.
explanations.B: 1 in 5 overstates how often pairs come.
explanations.C: 1 in 221 is the chance of one specific pair.
explanations.D: 1 in 1326 is a single exact combo.

questions.question: How many distinct two-card combinations make a specific pocket pair (such as KK)?

explanations.A: Two undercounts the suit pairings.
explanations.B: Correct. Choosing 2 of the 4 kings gives 6 combinations.
explanations.C: Four is the count for a suited non-pair hand.
explanations.D: 12 is the offsuit count for a non-pair hand.

questions.question: How many combinations of A-K suited (any suit) exist?

explanations.A: One would be a single exact card combo, not all suited versions.
explanations.B: Six is the count for a pocket pair.
explanations.C: Correct. One per suit gives 4 suited combinations of A-K.
explanations.D: 12 is the offsuit count of A-K.

questions.question: How many combinations of A-K offsuit are there?

explanations.A: Four is the suited count.
explanations.B: Six is a pocket-pair count.
explanations.C: 16 is all A-K combos, suited and offsuit together.
explanations.D: Correct. 4 aces times 4 kings minus 4 suited equals 12 offsuit combos.

questions.question: Counting suited and offsuit, how many A-K combinations are there in total?

explanations.A: Correct. 4 aces times 4 kings equals 16 total A-K combos.
explanations.B: Four is suited only.
explanations.C: 12 is offsuit only.
explanations.D: 78 is the number of distinct suited hands overall.

questions.question: How many distinct two-card starting combinations exist when suits are counted?

explanations.A: 169 is the number of strategically distinct hands, not raw combos.
explanations.B: Correct. The count of 2 cards chosen from 52 is 1326.
explanations.C: 2652 double-counts order, 52 times 51.
explanations.D: 52 is just the size of the deck.

questions.question: How many strategically distinct starting hands are there (pairs, suited, and offsuit)?

explanations.A: 1326 counts every suit combination separately.
explanations.B: 78 is just the suited (or just the offsuit) group.
explanations.C: Correct. 13 pairs plus 78 suited plus 78 offsuit equals 169 distinct hands.
explanations.D: 52 is the size of the deck.

questions.question: Holding a pocket pair, your chance of flopping a set or better is about:

explanations.A: 1% drastically understates it.
explanations.B: 33% is far too high.
explanations.C: 50% is wildly optimistic.
explanations.D: Correct. You flop a set about 11.8% of the time, near 7.5-to-1 against.

questions.question: Your 'equity' in a pot is:
options: Your share of the pot based on your chance to win
The chips you have already bet

explanations.A: Correct. Equity is your expected share given your probability of winning.
explanations.B: Money already bet is sunk, not equity.
explanations.C: Stack size is separate from pot equity.
explanations.D: Rake is a fee, not equity.

questions.question: A 'coinflip' or 'race' (such as a pair versus two overcards) is a spot where each side has roughly:

explanations.A: 90% would not be a flip at all.
explanations.B: Correct. A race is close to 50/50, often around 52-48.
explanations.C: 10% describes a heavy underdog.
explanations.D: 0% means drawing dead.

questions.question: 'Drawing dead' means:
options: You currently have the best hand
No remaining card can make you the winner

explanations.A: If you already hold the best hand, you are not drawing dead.
explanations.B: It describes your equity, not a forced fold.
explanations.C: Correct. Drawing dead means no card can give you a winning hand.
explanations.D: You can be all-in and still very much live.

questions.question: The strongest starting hand in Hold'em is:

explanations.A: A-K suited is strong but an underdog to a high pair preflop.
explanations.B: Pocket kings lose to pocket aces.
explanations.C: A-K offsuit is weaker than any high pair preflop.
explanations.D: Correct. Pocket aces (A-A) is the best preflop hand.

questions.question: Compared with its offsuit version, a suited hand is:
options: Slightly stronger because of its flush potential

explanations.A: Correct. Suited cards add flush equity, a small but real edge.
explanations.B: A suited hand is not worse than its offsuit version.
explanations.C: The flush potential makes them non-identical.
explanations.D: The suited edge applies across all formats.

questions.question: Connected cards like 8-9 make straights more easily than 8-5 mainly because they:
options: Have no gaps, allowing the most straight combinations

explanations.A: Rank height is not the reason here.
explanations.B: Correct. Gapless connectors can complete the most straights.
explanations.C: Connectedness is independent of being suited.
explanations.D: Counterfeiting is a separate concept.

questions.question: The 'nuts' refers to:
options: The best possible hand given the board

explanations.A: The worst hand is the opposite of the nuts.
explanations.B: A flush may or may not be the nuts.
explanations.C: Correct. The nuts is the strongest hand possible on a given board.
explanations.D: A bluff is a betting action, not a hand.

questions.question: A 'bluff' is:
options: Betting a strong hand to get called
Betting or raising a weak hand to make opponents fold

explanations.A: Betting a strong hand for calls is value betting.
explanations.B: Checking the nuts is a trap, not a bluff.
explanations.C: Folding the best hand is a mistake, not a bluff.
explanations.D: Correct. A bluff bets a weak hand to fold out better hands.

questions.question: A 'value bet' aims to:
options: Win the pot without a showdown

explanations.A: Correct. A value bet wants calls from weaker hands.
explanations.B: Folding out better hands is bluffing.
explanations.C: Winning without showdown is the goal of a bluff.
explanations.D: Side pots come from all-ins, not from bet intent.

questions.question: A 'semi-bluff' is a bet made with:
options: A weak hand that can still improve, such as a draw
A hand with no equity at all

explanations.A: Betting the nuts is pure value, not a semi-bluff.
explanations.B: Correct. A semi-bluff bets a draw that can win now by a fold or later by improving.
explanations.C: A pure bluff has little equity; a semi-bluff has draw equity.
explanations.D: Semi-bluffs specifically involve drawing hands.

questions.question: A 'cooler' describes a situation where:
options: You fold the best hand by mistake
Two very strong hands clash and a big loss is hard to avoid

explanations.A: A cooler is not primarily about misplay.
explanations.B: Folding the best hand is a different kind of error.
explanations.C: Correct. A cooler is when strong hands collide and losing is nearly unavoidable.
explanations.D: A 'brick' is a blank card, which is not a cooler.

questions.question: 'Tilt' refers to:
options: Emotional play that leads to poor decisions

explanations.A: Tilt is the opposite of focused control.
explanations.B: Tilt is a mental state, not a hand.
explanations.C: Tilt is not a betting structure.
explanations.D: Correct. Tilt is letting emotion degrade your decision-making.

questions.question: The 'rake' is:
options: A fee the house takes from pots or tournament entries
A bonus paid to the winner
Another name for the big blind

explanations.A: Correct. The rake is the house's fee for hosting the game.
explanations.B: Rake is taken by the house, not given as a bonus.
explanations.C: The big blind is a forced bet, not a fee.
explanations.D: Rake is unrelated to going all-in.

questions.question: A key difference in cash games is that:
options: Chips have direct cash value and you can rebuy or leave anytime
There are no blinds at all

explanations.A: Rising blinds are a tournament feature.
explanations.B: Correct. Cash chips equal money, so you can buy in, top up, or cash out freely.
explanations.C: Busting in a cash game just means rebuying.
explanations.D: Cash games still post blinds.

questions.question: In a freezeout tournament, losing all your chips means:

explanations.A: A freezeout does not allow rebuys.
explanations.B: Blinds keep rising regardless of eliminations.
explanations.C: Correct. In a freezeout, reaching zero chips means elimination.
explanations.D: There is no refund for busting.

questions.question: The Independent Chip Model (ICM) reflects that:
options: Chips always equal straight cash value
Only the chip leader can cash
Chip stacks map to real-money equity in a nonlinear way

explanations.A: Tournament chips do not equal linear cash value.
explanations.B: Blinds still drive ICM pressure.
explanations.C: Many players cash, not just the leader.
explanations.D: Correct. ICM ties stacks to prize equity, so each extra chip is worth a bit less.

questions.question: Playing more hands from late position is justified because you:
options: Act last postflop and can play more profitably
Are simply forced to play more
Never have to post any blinds

explanations.A: Correct. Acting last lets you realize equity better and widen your profitable range.
explanations.B: It is a choice based on edge, not a requirement.
explanations.C: Card distribution is random regardless of seat.
explanations.D: You still pay the blinds as they rotate to you.

questions.question: Why are the blinds generally losing positions over the long run?
options: They invest forced chips and then act out of position postflop
They are not allowed to raise

explanations.A: Every seat is dealt the same number of cards.
explanations.B: Correct. The blinds put money in blind and then act out of position postflop.
explanations.C: Card quality is random by seat.
explanations.D: The blinds can raise; that is not the issue.

questions.question: Acting out of turn is discouraged because it:
options: Gives away information and can be binding if the action does not change

explanations.A: It is corrected, not punished by elimination.
explanations.B: It does not award the pot.
explanations.C: Correct. Out-of-turn action leaks information and may be held if the action to you is unchanged.
explanations.D: It does not reset earlier betting.

questions.question: Facing a bet, silently placing a single oversized chip without saying 'raise' is generally:
options: A raise to that chip's full value

explanations.A: Without announcing a raise, one chip is treated as a call.
explanations.B: Putting a chip out as a call is legal, not illegal.
explanations.C: Putting chips forward is not a fold.
explanations.D: Correct. A single oversized chip with no announced raise counts as a call.

questions.question: If you release your cards into the muck and they touch the discards, your hand is:
options: Dead; you cannot retrieve or play it

explanations.A: Correct. Once your cards hit the muck, the hand is dead.
explanations.B: A mucked hand is no longer live.
explanations.C: A dead hand wins nothing.
explanations.D: Mucking forfeits any claim to the pot.

questions.question: At most casinos, if you say 'call' but the standing bet was a raise, you are bound to:

explanations.A: Saying 'call' does not let you fold for free.
explanations.B: Correct. Saying 'call' commits you to the full current bet, including the raise.
explanations.C: A call is not a raise.
explanations.D: A verbal call is binding; you cannot simply retract it.

questions.question: When the board pairs (for example K-K-7), it becomes possible for a player to hold:
options: A flush from the pair alone

explanations.A: A paired board does not by itself enable a flush.
explanations.B: Straight flushes need suited connectors, not a pair.
explanations.C: Correct. A pair on the board allows trips and full houses.
explanations.D: Trips and boats beat one pair, so better hands exist.

questions.question: Three cards of the same suit on the board mean a flush is:
options: Only possible with a pocket pair
Possible if a player holds two cards of that suit

explanations.A: Three suited cards make a flush very possible.
explanations.B: Three suited board cards are not yet a flush for anyone.
explanations.C: Flushes are about suits, not pairs.
explanations.D: Correct. A player with two cards of that suit completes a flush.

questions.question: A board of 9-T-J means a straight is possible for someone holding:

explanations.A: Correct. Cards such as Q-8, K-Q, or 8-7 complete a straight with 9-T-J.
explanations.B: Two pair is not a straight.
explanations.C: A flush draw is a different kind of draw.
explanations.D: Pocket deuces make at best a pair here.

questions.question: Your two pair (aces and fives) can be 'counterfeited' when:
options: The board pairs higher, demoting your second pair on kickers

explanations.A: Hitting a flush improves you rather than counterfeiting you.
explanations.B: Correct. A higher pair on the board can outrank your lower pair and weaken your hand.
explanations.C: An opponent folding does not change your hand's rank.
explanations.D: Going all-in does not change card values.

questions.question: The expected value (EV) of a decision is:
options: The most you could possibly win
The probability-weighted average outcome over the long run
The size of the current pot

explanations.A: EV is an average, not the best-case result.
explanations.B: Stack size is not EV.
explanations.C: Correct. EV weights each outcome by its probability and sums them.
explanations.D: Pot size alone is not EV.

questions.question: A '+EV' play is one that:
options: Profits on average over many repetitions

explanations.A: +EV does not guarantee winning any single hand.
explanations.B: A +EV play can lose individual hands while profiting long-term.
explanations.C: Nothing guarantees winning a single pot.
explanations.D: Correct. A +EV decision earns money on average across many trials.

questions.question: 'Variance' in poker describes:
options: The short-term swings around your expected results
The rake taken from the pot

explanations.A: Correct. Variance is the up-and-down swing around your expectation.
explanations.B: The long-term win rate is the EV trend, not variance.
explanations.C: Rake is a fee, not variance.
explanations.D: Blind structure is unrelated to variance.

questions.question: Proper bankroll management mainly helps a player:

explanations.A: No strategy wins every session.
explanations.B: Correct. A bankroll buffer lets you ride out variance without busting.
explanations.C: You still pay blinds regardless of bankroll.
explanations.D: Bankrolling manages, but cannot remove, variance.

questions.question: Implied odds account for:
options: Only the chips already in the pot
Extra chips you expect to win on later streets if you hit

explanations.A: Current-pot-only thinking is plain pot odds.
explanations.B: The rake is not part of implied odds.
explanations.C: Correct. Implied odds add the future bets you expect to collect when you improve.
explanations.D: You estimate ranges, not exact cards.

questions.question: Reverse implied odds warn that:
options: You will always win when you hit
You may lose extra chips even when you make your hand

explanations.A: Hitting does not guarantee a win against a better hand.
explanations.B: Draws can pay off; this concept is about the downside.
explanations.C: The pot grows as players bet.
explanations.D: Correct. You can complete your hand yet still pay off a stronger one.

questions.question: A 'continuation bet' (c-bet) is when the preflop raiser:
options: Bets the flop to continue their preflop aggression

explanations.A: Correct. The preflop aggressor bets the flop to keep up the pressure.
explanations.B: Folding is not a continuation bet.
explanations.C: Checking is the opposite of c-betting.
explanations.D: Calling is passive and is not a c-bet.

questions.question: A '3-bet' preflop is:
options: A re-raise over the first raise

explanations.A: The big blind is the forced bet, counted as the first 'bet.'
explanations.B: Correct. A re-raise over the opening raise is the 3-bet.
explanations.C: The first raise is the 2-bet, the open.
explanations.D: A call is not a raise.

questions.question: A '4-bet' preflop is:
options: A bet made on the flop

explanations.A: The first open raise is the 2-bet.
explanations.B: Flop betting is a separate street.
explanations.C: Correct. A 4-bet re-raises a 3-bet.
explanations.D: A check adds no chips.

questions.question: To 'limp' preflop means to:
options: Just call the big blind without raising

explanations.A: Limping is the opposite of raising.
explanations.B: Folding exits the hand.
explanations.C: Going all-in is a maximal raise, not a limp.
explanations.D: Correct. Limping is entering the pot by only calling the big blind.

questions.question: A typical preflop 'open' is a:
options: Raise to roughly 2 to 3 times the big blind

explanations.A: Correct. Opens are commonly 2 to 3 times the big blind, larger with limpers.
explanations.B: Calling the big blind is a limp, not an open-raise.
explanations.C: You cannot check to open when facing the big blind.
explanations.D: Folding exits the hand.

questions.question: A 'dry' board such as K-7-2 rainbow is one that:
options: Connects with few draws and is hard to improve against

explanations.A: Lots of draws describe a wet board.
explanations.B: Correct. A dry board offers few straight or flush draws.
explanations.C: All one suit is a monotone, wet board.
explanations.D: A rainbow board cannot contain a flush.

questions.question: A 'wet' board such as 9-8-6 with two of a suit is dangerous because it:
options: Connects with many straight and flush draws

explanations.A: Wet boards are full of draws.
explanations.B: It does not guarantee anyone the nuts.
explanations.C: Correct. Coordinated, suited boards enable many drawing hands.
explanations.D: Betting is allowed on any board.

questions.question: Holding the ace of spades on a three-spade board gives you a 'blocker' because it:
options: Lowers the chance an opponent holds the nut flush

explanations.A: A single ace alone is not a made flush.
explanations.B: A blocker does not auto-win the pot.
explanations.C: It is a live, useful card, not dead.
explanations.D: Correct. The ace of spades blocks the nut flush, reducing the odds an opponent has it.

questions.question: Thinking in 'ranges' means you:
options: Consider all the hands an opponent could plausibly hold
Try to guess one exact hand

explanations.A: Correct. Range-based thinking weighs every plausible holding.
explanations.B: Pinning one exact hand is usually too narrow.
explanations.C: Ranges are about reading opponents, not ignoring them.
explanations.D: Counting outs is a separate calculation.

questions.question: A 'polarized' betting range consists mostly of:

explanations.A: Medium hands define a 'merged,' not polarized, range.
explanations.B: Correct. A polarized range pairs strong value hands with bluffs.
explanations.C: Draws alone are not a complete polarized range.
explanations.D: A polarized range includes bluffs too, not just the nuts.

questions.question: Game Theory Optimal (GTO) play aims to be:
options: Unexploitable by balancing value bets and bluffs

explanations.A: Maximal exploitation is the opposite, exploitative play.
explanations.B: GTO mixes actions; it is not always aggressive.
explanations.C: Correct. GTO seeks balanced frequencies that opponents cannot exploit.
explanations.D: GTO is not uniformly passive either.

questions.question: Exploitative play means you:
options: Always play exactly the same way
Deviate from balance to punish opponents' specific leaks

explanations.A: Exploitative play focuses on opponent tendencies.
explanations.B: Adjusting is the whole point; you change your strategy.
explanations.C: It is not a fold-only approach.
explanations.D: Correct. You adjust to attack predictable folding, calling, or sizing errors.

questions.question: 'Balancing' your range means:
options: Mixing bluffs with value so your bets are not predictable

explanations.A: Correct. Balance blends bluffs and value to keep you unpredictable.
explanations.B: Value-only bets are easy to read.
explanations.C: An all-bluff range is just as exploitable.
explanations.D: Balance is about bet composition, not blanket folding.

questions.question: Choosing a larger bet on a draw-heavy board often aims to:
options: Give opponents a great price to chase
Charge draws and protect your made hand

explanations.A: Small bets give draws cheap cards, the opposite goal.
explanations.B: Correct. Larger bets make drawing hands pay a worse price to continue.
explanations.C: Bigger bets build the pot rather than slow it.
explanations.D: Sizing for protection is not mainly about inducing bluffs.

questions.question: 'Fold equity' is the value you gain from:
options: The chance your opponent folds to your bet

explanations.A: Being always called means no fold equity.
explanations.B: Hitting a draw is card equity, not fold equity.
explanations.C: Correct. Fold equity is the added value from opponents folding.
explanations.D: Blinds are forced bets, unrelated to fold equity.

questions.question: A standard full-ring Hold'em table seats up to:

explanations.A: 6 players is 'six-max,' a shorter table.
explanations.B: Two players is heads-up.
explanations.C: 15 exceeds a standard table and a safe deal from one deck.
explanations.D: Correct. Full ring typically seats 9 or 10 players.

questions.question: 'Six-max' refers to a table with a maximum of:

explanations.A: Correct. Six-max seats up to six players.
explanations.B: Four would be a shorter table than six-max.
explanations.C: Eight exceeds the six-max maximum.
explanations.D: Ten players is full ring.

questions.question: After each hand, the dealer button moves:

explanations.A: The button moves clockwise, not counter-clockwise.
explanations.B: Correct. The button advances one seat clockwise each hand.
explanations.C: Its movement is fixed, not random.
explanations.D: The button rotates rather than staying fixed.

questions.question: In heads-up play, the player on the button posts:

explanations.A: Heads-up, the non-button player posts the big blind.
explanations.B: A blind is still posted by the button heads-up.
explanations.C: Correct. Heads-up, the button posts the small blind.
explanations.D: Each player posts one blind, not both.

questions.question: Two unpaired overcards (such as A-K) against a lower pair (such as Q-Q) preflop are roughly:

explanations.A: The overcards are not a favorite here.
explanations.B: A-K can pair up or make a straight, so it is not dead.
explanations.C: It is close to, but not exactly, 50%.
explanations.D: Correct. A-K versus Q-Q is about a 43 to 48% underdog, close to a coinflip.

questions.question: A pocket pair against two undercards (such as J-J versus 9-8) is about:

explanations.A: Correct. The overpair is roughly an 80% favorite.
explanations.B: It is clearly better than a coinflip.
explanations.C: The pair is the favorite, not the underdog.
explanations.D: The undercards have outs, but the pair dominates.

questions.question: A hand is 'dominated' when it shares a card with a stronger hand, like A-Q versus A-K. The A-Q is:
options: A big underdog, often around 25 to 30%

explanations.A: The shared ace with a worse kicker makes A-Q the underdog.
explanations.B: Correct. Dominated by the better kicker, A-Q is only about 25 to 30% against A-K.
explanations.C: Domination makes A-Q worse than even money.
explanations.D: A-Q can still hit a queen or a straight, so it is not dead.

questions.question: If a misdeal is declared before significant action, the dealer:
options: Pays a penalty to all players
Awards the pot to the button

explanations.A: A misdeal is corrected, not penalized to players.
explanations.B: No pot is awarded on a misdeal.
explanations.C: Correct. A misdeal voids the deal, and the cards are reshuffled and dealt again.
explanations.D: Play does not continue on a confirmed misdeal.

questions.question: If a card is accidentally exposed during the deal, it is typically:
options: Handled by house rule, often replaced or used as a burn

explanations.A: Exposed deal cards are corrected, not simply kept.
explanations.B: Exposed cards are not awarded to the button.
explanations.C: An accidental deal card does not become a community card.
explanations.D: Correct. House rules govern exposed cards, commonly replacing them or using them as a burn.

questions.question: Having 'the initiative' in a hand usually means you:
options: Were the last player to bet or raise

explanations.A: Correct. Initiative belongs to the last aggressor in the hand.
explanations.B: Calling is passive and does not give you the initiative.
explanations.C: Folding exits the hand entirely.
explanations.D: Checking does not seize the initiative.

questions.question: A hand with 'showdown value' is one that:
options: Can win at showdown unimproved, so checking is reasonable

explanations.A: Hands with showdown value usually prefer checking over bluffing.
explanations.B: Correct. Such hands can win without betting, so passive lines are often fine.
explanations.C: The nuts is a maximum, not merely 'showdown value.'
explanations.D: No-equity hands lack showdown value by definition.

questions.question: The 'one player to a hand' rule means:
options: Only one player can ever win
Only one card is dealt to each
You must make your own decisions without help during a hand
Only one bet is allowed per hand

explanations.A: Multiple players can be eligible; this rule is about assistance.
explanations.B: Each player is dealt two cards, not one.
explanations.C: Correct. You may not receive help or discuss your live hand with others.
explanations.D: Multiple bets are allowed across the streets.

questions.question: Discussing the contents of a live hand while others are still deciding is:
options: Against the rules because it can influence the action

explanations.A: It is discouraged, not encouraged.
explanations.B: It is prohibited, not required.
explanations.C: No chips are awarded for table talk.
explanations.D: Correct. Talking about a live hand can unfairly influence the action.

questions.question: An all-in player who is later outdrawn:
options: Owes nothing beyond the chips already committed

explanations.A: Correct. Under table stakes you can never lose more than the chips you put in that hand.
explanations.B: Table stakes cap losses at the chips committed.
explanations.C: Rebuying is a separate, optional choice.
explanations.D: Being outdrawn means losing that pot.

questions.question: Holding two suited cards, your chance of flopping a flush (three more of your suit) is about:

explanations.A: 10% is closer to flopping a flush draw, not a made flush.
explanations.B: Correct. Flopping a made flush with two suited cards is roughly 0.8%, about 118-to-1.
explanations.C: 35% is a flush draw's chance to complete by the river.
explanations.D: 50% is wildly too high.

questions.question: The chance that your two hole cards are suited is about:

explanations.A: 6% is far too low.
explanations.B: 50% overstates how often you are dealt suited cards.
explanations.C: Correct. Any two suited is 312 of 1326, about 23.5%, or roughly 1 in 4.
explanations.D: 75% is far too high.

questions.question: Holding two suited cards, your chance of flopping a flush draw (two more of your suit) is about:

explanations.A: 35% is the chance that such a draw then completes by the river.
explanations.B: 1% is closer to flopping a made flush.
explanations.C: 50% overstates it.
explanations.D: Correct. You flop a four-card flush draw about 10 to 11% of the time.

questions.question: When a straddle is posted, preflop action then opens with:
options: The player to the left of the straddle

explanations.A: Correct. Action begins to the left of the straddle, and the straddler keeps last action.
explanations.B: The straddler acts last preflop, like a larger blind.
explanations.C: The big blind already posted; action passes the straddle.
explanations.D: The button is not necessarily the first to act.

questions.question: A 'donk bet' is when a player:
options: Leads out into the previous street's aggressor from out of position

explanations.A: A preflop raise is simply an open or re-raise.
explanations.B: Correct. A donk bet leads into the prior aggressor from out of position.
explanations.C: Folding the river is not a donk bet.
explanations.D: Checking the nuts is a trap, not a donk bet.

questions.question: To 'float' is to:
options: Call a bet with a weak hand intending to take the pot later

explanations.A: Floating is the opposite of folding.
explanations.B: A float is a call, not an all-in.
explanations.C: Correct. A float calls now, planning to win the pot on a later street.
explanations.D: A check-raise is a different play.

questions.question: An 'overbet' is a bet that is:
options: Larger than the current size of the pot

explanations.A: A bet smaller than the pot is a standard or small bet.
explanations.B: The big blind is unrelated to overbet sizing.
explanations.C: Overbets can be for value or as a bluff.
explanations.D: Correct. An overbet exceeds the size of the pot.

questions.question: 'Set over set,' where your set loses to a bigger set, is a classic example of:

explanations.A: Correct. Set over set is a textbook cooler, a strong hand losing to a stronger one.
explanations.B: It is the opposite of a guaranteed win.
explanations.C: No bluffing is involved in set over set.
explanations.D: It is a clash of made hands, not a simple fold.

questions.question: Blinds serve a purpose similar to the 'bring-in' in stud by:
options: Forcing initial money into the pot to create action

explanations.A: Neither blinds nor the bring-in reveal hole cards.
explanations.B: Correct. Both are forced bets that seed the pot and spur action.
explanations.C: They start the hand, not end it.
explanations.D: The rake is set separately by the house.

questions.question: On the flop with 5 outs to improve, your approximate chance to hit by the river is:

explanations.A: 5% badly understates 5 outs over two cards.
explanations.B: 50% is far too high for only 5 outs.
explanations.C: Correct. Using the rule of 4, 5 outs times 4 is about 20%.
explanations.D: 80% is unrealistic for this draw.

questions.question: When neither player is all-in, equity is best thought of as:
options: Your range's equity against your opponent's range

explanations.A: Single-hand matchups ignore the uncertainty about holdings.
explanations.B: Equity is rarely an even 50/50.
explanations.C: Chip counts do not set hand equity.
explanations.D: Correct. Decisions weigh range-versus-range equity, not a single matchup.

questions.question: In fixed-limit games, the number of raises per street is usually:
options: Capped, commonly a bet plus three or four raises

explanations.A: Correct. Limit games cap raises, often at a bet plus three or four raises.
explanations.B: Limit betting is capped, not unlimited.
explanations.C: More than one raise is allowed, up to the cap.
explanations.D: The cap is a fixed rule, not arbitrary.

questions.question: When a limit pot becomes heads-up between two players, the raise cap is often:

explanations.A: Heads-up loosens, not tightens, the cap.
explanations.B: Correct. Many rooms lift the raise cap once the pot is heads-up.
explanations.C: It is uncapped, not limited to one raise.
explanations.D: It applies to the two players still in, not the entire table.

questions.question: Standard Texas Hold'em is a 'high' game, meaning the pot goes to:
options: A split between high and low always

explanations.A: Low hands win in split or lowball games, not standard Hold'em.
explanations.B: Hold'em is not a split high-low game by default.
explanations.C: Correct. In high-only Hold'em, the best (highest) hand wins the pot.
explanations.D: Action order does not award the pot.

questions.question: 'Equity realization' improves when you:
options: Play in position with the ability to control the pot

explanations.A: Out of position usually realizes less equity.
explanations.B: Over-folding sacrifices equity you could have realized.
explanations.C: Always shoving ignores position and skill edges.
explanations.D: Correct. Position helps you realize more of your raw equity.

questions.question: A 'time bank' in tournaments gives a player:
options: Extra time to make a difficult decision

explanations.A: Correct. A time bank is reserve time for tough decisions.
explanations.B: A time bank grants time, not chips.
explanations.C: It is unrelated to rebuys.
explanations.D: It does not act for you automatically.

questions.question: The 'dead button' rule, used when a blind player busts, results in:
options: No button being used that hand
The button resting on an empty seat so blinds stay fair
Two buttons in play at once

explanations.A: There is still a button position, even if on an empty seat.
explanations.B: Correct. A dead button preserves fair blind rotation after a player leaves.
explanations.C: There is only ever one button.
explanations.D: The streets are unaffected by the dead button.

questions.question: 'Defending your big blind' means:
options: Calling or raising to contest a steal attempt

explanations.A: Always folding surrenders the blind without a fight.
explanations.B: You post the blind once per orbit in that seat.
explanations.C: Correct. Defending means calling or re-raising to fight for the pot.
explanations.D: Mucking face up is unrelated to defending.

questions.question: A 'steal' attempt is typically a late-position raise aimed at:
options: Building a huge pot with the nuts

explanations.A: A steal is not necessarily made with a premium hand.
explanations.B: A steal is an aggressive raise, not a slow-play.
explanations.C: Raising to steal is the opposite of folding.
explanations.D: Correct. A steal tries to pick up the blinds when the action folds to late position.

questions.question: A 'squeeze' play is a preflop re-raise made after:
options: An open-raise and one or more callers

explanations.A: Correct. A squeeze re-raises an opener plus caller(s), leveraging the dead money.
explanations.B: With everyone folded there is nothing to squeeze.
explanations.C: A squeeze is a preflop play here.
explanations.D: It follows a raise and calls, not a check.

questions.question: For very large draws (about 15 outs), the rule of 4 overestimates, so true equity is closer to:

explanations.A: 15 outs is strong but not over 90% by the river.
explanations.B: Correct. Around 15 outs is roughly 54% by the river, less than 15 times 4 equals 60%.
explanations.C: 15% badly understates a 15-out monster.
explanations.D: It is far from drawing dead.

questions.question: Before continuing in any pot, the core question fundamentals teach you to ask is:
options: Am I getting the right price, and does my position help me control the pot?
How many chips are on the table in total?

explanations.A: Identifying the dealer is trivia, not the key decision.
explanations.B: Rake matters for game selection, not this in-hand decision.
explanations.C: Correct. Price (pot odds) plus position drive sound fundamental decisions.
explanations.D: Total table chips do not determine your specific choice.
